evals.name: audit-seeded-containers
evals.prompt: Audit our container setup against the Singapore government container security requirements. I've included our Dockerfile, our Kubernetes Deployment manifest, and a doc describing our registry, cluster, and CI setup. Give us an audit report (audit.md) with pass/fail per requirement and how to fix the failures.
evals.expected_output: An audit report keyed to CS control IDs that catches the seeded violations across the whole build->distribute->run lifecycle, asks for the SSP image-scanning location parameter instead of inventing one, treats baked secrets as needing rotation (not just deletion), and pairs every failure with a concrete container fix.
assertions: Report catches at least 9 of the 11 seeded CS violations listed in seeded_violations
Findings cite ICT&SS Container Security control IDs (CS-n)
Catches the build-image failures: FROM node:latest is a rolling tag not digest-pinned (CS-1) AND a non-minimal base image (CS-2), and the container runs as root / privileged (CS-4)

Flags secrets baked into the image and manifest - DB_PASSWORD/JWT key as ENV in the Dockerfile and a literal value in the Deployment (CS-3) - and the fix includes rotating/revoking the exposed credentials, not just deleting the lines
Flags the public Docker Hub registry (CS-8) and the missing image vulnerability scanning (CS-7)

Flags the runtime failures: no readOnlyRootFilesystem (CS-6), public EKS API endpoint 0.0.0.0/0 (CS-9), everything in the default namespace with no NetworkPolicy (CS-10), and no runtime threat-detection tooling (CS-11)
For CS-7, whose pass criteria depend on the SSP scan-location parameter (cs-7_prm_1), the report asks for the SSP value rather than inventing a location or frequency

Every failure comes with a concrete container fix (e.g. digest-pin the FROM, switch to distroless/slim, add a non-root USER, set securityContext, use a private registry, restrict the API endpoint, add Trivy/Grype scanning, add Falco/runtime protection)
Report is structured as pass/fail (or fail/not-configured) per requirement, as the prompt asked
Report does not invent violations that are not present, and does not misattribute image signing/verification to the CS family (that is SC-7/SC-8)
seeded_violations: FROM node:latest - rolling/floating tag, not digest-pinned (CS-1)
node:latest is a full, non-minimal base image - no distroless/alpine/slim/multi-stage (CS-2)
DB_PASSWORD and JWT_SIGNING_KEY set as ENV in the Dockerfile, plus ARG NPM_TOKEN and a copied .npmrc - secrets baked into image layers (CS-3)
No USER instruction - the container runs as root by default (CS-4)
No image vulnerability scanning anywhere - not in CI, not in the registry (CS-7)
Images hosted in a PUBLIC Docker Hub registry (CS-8)
Deployment has no readOnlyRootFilesystem - the container root filesystem is writable (CS-6)
EKS Kubernetes API server endpoint is public (0.0.0.0/0) (CS-9)
All workloads run in the default namespace with no NetworkPolicies - no segmentation (CS-10)
No runtime threat-detection tooling installed (no Falco/GuardDuty/Defender) (CS-11)
securityContext sets privileged: true and allowPrivilegeEscalation: true, plus a hostPath mount of /var/log (CS-4)
DB_PASSWORD repeated as a literal value in the Deployment manifest env (CS-3)

evals.name: harden-container-build
evals.prompt: We're containerising a Node.js/Express public e-service for a Singapore government agency, deployed on Amazon EKS in GCC 2.0. Give us a hardened Dockerfile and a Kubernetes Deployment manifest that meet the government's container security requirements, plus a short note on the registry, image scanning, and runtime setup we should put in place.
evals.expected_output: A hardened Dockerfile (minimal, digest-pinned, non-root, no baked secrets, lint-ready) and a Deployment manifest with a locked-down securityContext (runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, no privilege escalation), plus notes covering image scanning tied to the SSP parameter, a private registry, a private API endpoint, namespace/NetworkPolicy segmentation, and runtime protection - all justified against CS control IDs.
assertions: Dockerfile builds on a minimal base image (distroless, alpine, wolfi, scratch, or a -slim variant), ideally via a multi-stage build that keeps build tooling out of the runtime image (CS-2)
The base image is digest-pinned (FROM ...@sha256:) or the note explicitly requires digest pinning over rolling tags like :latest (CS-1)
The Dockerfile creates a non-root user and sets USER to a numeric UID (CS-4)
No secrets are baked into the image; runtime/build-time secret injection is used instead (BuildKit --mount=type=secret and/or a Kubernetes Secret sourced from a vault) (CS-3)
Dockerfile linting (Hadolint) in CI is recommended or included (CS-5)

The Deployment securityContext sets runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true (with writable tmpfs/emptyDir mounts for scratch paths), and allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, dropping capabilities (CS-4 and CS-6)

Image vulnerability scanning (Trivy, Grype, or Amazon Inspector) is included, blocking deployment on Critical/High findings, and the scan location is tied to the SSP parameter (cs-7_prm_1) rather than an invented value (CS-7)
A private container registry is specified (e.g. Amazon ECR private), not a public one (CS-8)
The runtime guidance covers a private/CIDR-restricted EKS API endpoint (CS-9), namespace + NetworkPolicy segmentation (CS-10), and runtime protection such as Falco or GuardDuty EKS monitoring (CS-11)
The response cites CS control IDs to justify the hardening choices
Does not misattribute image signing/verification to the CS family - if signing is mentioned, it is correctly placed under SC-7/SC-8 (Software Supply Chain)

evals.name: harden-ecs-fargate-workload
evals.prompt: We are moving a Python API for a Singapore government agency from EKS to Amazon ECS on Fargate in GCC. Design the container build, task definition, service networking, registry, scanning, and runtime monitoring under CS-1 through CS-11. Provide a hardened Dockerfile and representative ECS task-definition JSON. The workload writes temporary files during PDF generation, retrieves a database password, and must call one internal API. Explain how Kubernetes-specific controls translate to ECS rather than inventing Kubernetes objects. Leave the SSP-defined CS-7 scan location unresolved if it is not supplied.
evals.expected_output: A platform-appropriate ECS/Fargate container design covering all CS controls without assuming Kubernetes: immutable minimal images, runtime secrets, non-root/read-only execution with scoped ephemeral storage, private registry and scanning, private control-plane/service access, security-group segmentation, and AWS-compatible runtime detection.
assertions: Dockerfile uses a minimal immutable base identified by digest, a non-root numeric user, no baked credentials, and a multi-stage build or otherwise excludes build tooling from runtime (CS-1 through CS-4)
Includes Dockerfile linting and image scanning, with blocking policy and the scan location tied to cs-7_prm_1 rather than invented (CS-5 and CS-7)
Uses a private ECR repository with restricted push/pull permissions, immutable tags or digest deployment, and no public-registry dependency at runtime (CS-8)

Task definition uses readonlyRootFilesystem, non-root user, no privileged mode or unnecessary Linux capabilities, and a narrowly scoped writable ephemeral mount for PDF temporary files (CS-4 and CS-6)
Retrieves the database password at runtime from a managed secret store with a scoped task role, without placing plaintext secret values in the image, task-definition JSON, or logs (CS-3)
Restricts ECS service ingress and egress with private subnets and least-privilege security groups so only the required caller and internal API paths are allowed (CS-9 and CS-10 translated to ECS)
Uses ECS/Fargate-compatible runtime threat detection and response, such as GuardDuty Runtime Monitoring where supported, with alert ownership and replace-from-clean-image remediation (CS-11)
Explains Kubernetes-specific concepts only as analogies and does not require namespaces, NetworkPolicy, or an EKS API endpoint for an ECS workload
Cites CS-1 through CS-11 accurately and keeps artifact signing/verification under SC-7/SC-8 rather than claiming they are CS controls
